Brunson Crisis This crisis, which started in September 2016 and lasted until October 12, 2018, has been a tense period in terms of bilateral relations between America and Turkey. The Brunson crisis, the tension in the relations between the two countries and the impact of the economic sanctions imposed on Turkey by the United States, caused a serious loss of value in the Turkish lira. The Brunson Crisis began in September 2016 when Andrew Craig Brunson, an American citizen and the pastor of the Resurrection Church in Izmir, Turkey for 23 years, and his wife Norine Lyn Brunson, were requested to be deported from Turkey on the grounds of "engaging in activities that threaten national security." On December 9, 2016, while the deportation process was ongoing, a secret witness provided a statement against Brunson. In the statement, it was alleged that Brunson had engaged in meetings with the Fethullahist Terrorist Organization (FETÖ) and expressed dissatisfaction with the unsuccessful coup attempt that took place in Turkey on July 15, 2016.  Following the given statement, Brunson was arrested and faced charges of "obtaining confidential information for political or military espionage purposes." He was initially faced with a prison sentence of 20 years, and a total of 35 years of imprisonment was requested. During the meeting between US President Donald Trump and Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan on May 16, 2017, the situation of Pastor Brunson was brought up. The White House stated, "President Trump urged for the swift return of Pastor Brunson to the United States." In response to this request from the White House, Recep Tayyip Erdoğan, during his speech at the Police Academy Graduation Ceremony on September 28, 2017, emphasized, "America is asking for the extradition of a pastor from us. You also have a pastor. Give him to us." He further  emphasized that Turkey would not release Pastor Brunson unless Washington extradited Fethullah Gülen to Turkey. It was claimed that Brunson, who had been in prison for 16 months while his indictment was being prepared, committed crimes on behalf of terrorist organizations under the guise of a priest and that he founded the "Kurdi Church of the Messiah" for this purpose. At the first hearing held on 16 April, the continuation of his detention was adjourned until 7 May. On April 18, 2018, in response to the rejection of Pastor Brunson's release request and his indictment on espionage charges, US President Donald Trump took to his Twitter account and posted the following message: "Pastor Andrew Brunson, a fine gentleman and Christian leader in the United States, is on trial and being persecuted in Turkey for no reason. They call him a Spy, but I am more a Spy than he is. Hopefully, he will be allowed to come home to his beautiful family where he belongs!"  With this statement, Trump emphasized Brunson's role as a Christian leader and portrayed Turkey as unjustly prosecuting him, aiming to create an image of unfair treatment towards Brunson. On April 27, 2018, three American senators, James Lankford, Jeanne Shaheen, and Thom Tillis, submitted a proposal to the US Senate in order to prevent the planned delivery of F-35 fighter jets to Turkey and impose sanctions. The purpose of this proposal was to hinder the delivery of F-35 aircraft as a means of applying pressure and sanctions on Turkey.  As a result of the senators' proposal, the issue of F-35 fighter jets started to be discussed in parallel with the Brunson case in the United States. During the second hearing held on May 7, secret witnesses claimed that Brunson was attempting to create a "Christian Kurdish State." Despite Brunson's denial of these allegations, his detention was continued, and the trial was postponed once again. Following the renewal of the White House's call for Brunson's release, Turkish Foreign Minister Mevlüt Çavuşoğlu responded by stating that it is not possible to intervene in the legal process and that the outcome of the court should be awaited in order to release the pastor.  On June 20, 2018, Turkish Deputy Prime Minister Bekir Bozdağ stated that the request for the release of American pastor Andrew Brunson, who was detained in relation to the Fethullahist  Terrorist Organization (FETÖ) investigation, in exchange for the delivery of F-35 jets , was considered a threat. During the third hearing on July 18, Andrew Brunson's request for release was once again denied, and the decision was made to continue his detention. The trial was adjourned until October 12. As a result of the decision to keep Brunson in custody, President Trump took to his Twitter account and stated, "A total disgrace that Turkey will not release a respected U.S. Pastor, Andrew Brunson, from prison. He has been held hostage far too long. @RT_Erdogan should do something to free this wonderful Christian husband & father. He has done nothing wrong, and his family needs him!"    In this statement, Trump referred to Brunson as a "hostage" and sought public support for his release. Additionally, the American Senate initiated efforts to prevent Turkey from accessing credit from international financial institutions. Due to Andrew Brunson's health issues, on July 25, it was decided that he would be placed under house arrest, allowing him to receive visitors and have access to the internet and communication devices. In response to this decision, US Secretary of State Mike Pompeo posted on his Twitter account, along with a photo of Andrew Brunson, stating, "We welcome long overdue news that Pastor Brunson has been moved from prison to house arrest in #Turkey, but it is not enough. We have seen no credible evidence against Mr. Brunson and call on Turkish authorities to resolve his case immediately in a transparent and fair manner."  Pompeo expressed satisfaction with Brunson being placed under house arrest but emphasized that this step was not sufficient and called for his release. Donald Trump also commented on a similar issue, stating, "The United States will impose large sanctions on Turkey for their long-time detainment of Pastor Andrew Brunson, a great Christian, family man, and wonderful human being. He is suffering greatly. This innocent man of faith should be released immediately!"  Trump highlighted that if Brunson is not released, severe sanctions will be imposed. In response to the sanction threats expressed by US authorities, Turkish Minister of Foreign Affairs Mevlüt Çavuşoğlu stated on his Twitter account, "No one dictates Turkey. We will never tolerate threats from anybody. Rule of law is for everyone; no exception."  With this statement, he emphasized that everyone is subject to the rule of law and reiterated Turkey's stance against external pressures and threats. As the crisis between the two countries escalated with the exchange of statements, the sense of insecurity created by the threat of sanctions also affected the markets. Russia-Ukraine  War The crisis between Russia and Ukraine, although it escalated into a war in early 2022, has periodically occupied a place in the international agenda since the early 1990s. Russia and Ukraine had been parts of the Soviet Union for over 70 years. After the dissolution of the USSR in 1991, Ukraine emerged as an independent state, always perceived as Russia's little brother and within its sphere of influence.  Despite the existence of such perceptions, Ukraine has faced difficulties in determining the direction of its foreign policy since gaining independence, struggling between pursuing a Western-oriented or Russia-leaning political stance.  Until 2004, Ukraine attempted to maintain a balance between the West and Russia through a policy of equilibrium. However, with the Orange Revolution in 2004, Ukraine prioritized the establishment of a pro-Western policy. Nevertheless, the failure to implement reforms that would sustain stability in the country after the Orange Revolution and the EU's limited offer of membership perspective, focusing instead on developing bilateral relations with Ukraine, were contributing factors to the political crisis that erupted in the country after 2013.  These factors brought about changes in the domestic political balance, and as a result, Ukraine, which had turned towards the West with the Orange Revolution, found itself in a political situation of dilemma between Russia and the EU with the election of Viktor Yanukovych, who was pro-Russia, as the president in the 2010 elections.  In the midst of this complex political landscape, Ukraine entered into a process of making a choice and, under the influence of Yanukovych, sought to improve its relations with Russia. Yanukovych, who favored closer relations with Russia instead of the West, played a role in suspending the signing of the Deep and Comprehensive Free Trade Area (DCFTA) with the EU, which was expected to be signed in 2013. This suspension, just 10 days before the expected signing, is seen as the main cause of the Ukraine Crisis.  After the announcement that Ukraine would not sign the agreement, protests began in Kiev in November 2013. These protests intensified over time, with increasing violence and casualties. Eventually, on February 22, 2014,  the protests led to Yanukovych fleeing to Russia and abandoning the country.  With Yanukovych's departure from office and the establishment of a pro-Western government, Ukraine entered a new era. During this new period, certain steps were taken that unsettled Russia, such as the repeal of the law granting regional language rights to Russian in Ukraine. These actions caused discomfort to Russia.  The crisis that had already begun escalated further with the reunification of Crimea by Russia, which internationalized the crisis. In February 2014, following the Crimean Parliament's decision to join Russia, a new pro-Russian autonomous government was established. This government announced that a referendum would be held to discuss the issue of independence.  Immediately after Crimea's reunification, pro-Russian protests began in the Donetsk and Luhansk regions of Ukraine, specifically in the Donbas region. Before long, these regions declared independence from Ukraine, with the establishment of the "Donetsk People's Republic" on April 7, 2014, and the "Luhansk People's Republic" on April 27, 2014.  These separatist movements, supported by Russia, have brought along years of ongoing conflicts. In February 2015, the Minsk Agreement was signed in the capital of Belarus, Minsk, between Ukraine, Russia, and the separatist leaders. The agreement aimed to achieve a ceasefire and included a decision to withdraw all heavy weapons. Efforts to find a solution to the crisis through diplomatic means have faced challenges in achieving tangible progress. Moreover, in recent days, the escalation of conflicts in the eastern part of the country has deepened the crisis. On February 21, 2022, Putin announced the recognition of independence for the two separatist regions of Ukraine, Donetsk and Luhansk.  Putin, in his address to the nation on the early morning of February 22, 2022, declared that the Minsk Agreement signed in 2015 was now null and void. He announced the initiation of a military operation in the Donbas region.  In his speech, Putin emphasized that this was not an occupation but a "special military operation" launched against Ukraine. He stated that the purpose of the military operation was to disarm Ukraine and rid it of neo-Nazis. On February 24, a war between Russia and Ukraine began. During this crisis, it can be observed that both Russia and Ukraine have engaged in spreading their state narratives, controlling the information environment, establishing good relations in the international community, and employing digital diplomacy in perception and image management. While the war between Russia and Ukraine has been dubbed the "TikTok War"  on social media, Twitter has been the platform used by states to inform the public and by users to spread misinformation. It is indeed evident that the Ukrainian government has frequently resorted to digital diplomacy in line with its objectives. Firstly, it can be noted that Ukraine has taken active measures to control the information environment. Controlling the information environment becomes an especially critical priority during times of war. In this process, with the aim of controlling the information environment and impeding Russia's state narratives, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky wrote a letter to Mark Zuckerberg, the CEO of Meta, the company that owns popular social media platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, in March 2022. In his letter, Zelensky requested the banning of Facebook and Instagram in Russia due to the military operation conducted by Russia.  In response to the situation, Nick Clegg, the Vice President of Global Affairs at Meta, publicly stated that they had significantly restricted media organizations affiliated with the Russian state. In retaliation, Russia imposed restrictions on social media platforms within its own territory and, in some cases, completely banned access to them. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, along with Ukrainian Minister for Digital Transformation Mykhailo Fedorov, publicly shared the letters they wrote to Apple , Samsung , and PayPal  via their Twitter accounts, urging them to cease their activities in Ukraine due to the military operation conducted by Russia. The disclosure of these letters and calls was not motivated  by a commitment to transparent governance or ensuring the constant exposure of personal information by social media users. Rather, these calls were made publicly to exert pressure on other actors. As these calls were made openly and gained support from both state and non-state actors, it became more challenging for major technology companies to reject Ukraine's demands. This facilitated Ukraine's dissemination of state narratives through social media platforms. In order to spread government narratives, the Ukrainian state has conducted an intensive digital diplomacy campaign on Twitter. In this context, President Zelensky, along with Foreign Minister Dymtro Kuleba and official state accounts, has actively engaged in digital diplomacy efforts. It can be said that Zelensky has been actively involved in this process, and the content he generates on Twitter has reached a large audience. Particularly, Zelensky's own Twitter account shared the message "Don't believe fakes,"  indicating that Zelenskyy and other national leaders did not flee from Kyiv. In another video, Zelensky is seen having breakfast with soldiers in an improvised tent, and in a third video, Zelenskyy states that he is in Kyiv and that Kyiv is standing strong. These interactions with users on Twitter by Zelensky have contributed to his increasing popularity. In January 2022, Zelensky had 400,000 followers, but by January 2023, this number had reached 7.1 million followers.   Throughout the process, Twitter users expressed their support using hashtags such as #Istandwithukraine, #ukraine, and #nowarinukraine, apart from the official Twitter accounts of Ukraine. Zelensky frequently connected digitally to the parliamentary sessions of world states and meetings of international organizations, both to convey government narratives and to seek assistance for his country. With the start of the war, Ukraine's official Twitter accounts called on Twitter users to financially support opposition against Russia. Ukraine's official Twitter account stated that they accepted crypto donations  and provided account details and bank accounts to enable direct contributions to the Ukrainian armed forces through digital channels. During this process, despite Russia's relatively limited presence on social media platforms, they attempted to connect their own narratives with the international community through their Twitter accounts in their digital diplomacy efforts. There are also instances where Russia has been successful in digital diplomacy practices. For example, the fate of Ukrainian soldiers stationed on Snake Island has been a significant digital diplomacy battle. Ukraine announced that these soldiers resisted Russia's surrender call and died fighting against Russian troops. However, in the following  days of the war, Russia presented footage claiming that the soldiers had surrendered to the international community's attention. 2.4. Chapter 3 Twiplomacy in terms of international crises: Trends and Challenges In this part of the study, it is also important to consider a current war as a case study in order to better understand what difficulties and trends occur during international crises. In this respect, it is important to evaluate digital diplomacy, which has been included in the history of diplomacy for centuries, within the context of a current war period. Since digital diplomacy is a new concept that is included in the international politics scene in the 21st century, examining digital diplomacy in a period of war will lead to the emergence of a study that aims to deal with a debate that has been going on throughout history under current conditions. For this reason, the Russia-Ukraine War, which started on February 24,  2022,  and is one of the largest and most comprehensive wars in the recent period, is an important empirical example. 3.1. The role of humour in Twiplomacy Humor has always played an important role in politics. In politics, humor is often used to criticize politicians and their policies, raise awareness in society, and educate people. Humor plays a significant role in international politics, especially in significant events. This is because it influences how international events and actors are communicated and understood. In today's world dominated by information and communication technologies, humor focuses on how the story is told and how well it reaches the target audience, rather than the reality of the event. By offering a simple, memorable, newsworthy, and widely appealing narrative, humor entertains while also informing, potentially becoming viral online. As pointed out by Corneliu Bjola, the use of humor, particularly satire, can be highly effective in reaching a larger audience, preventing questioning of the narrative, avoiding emotional escalation, and undermining the credibility of the source. The conflict between Russia and Ukraine, which began on February 24, 2022, has resulted in the emergence of new social media behaviors. One of these is the systematic use of strategic humor. Strategic humor refers to the deliberate use of humor by governments and affiliated groups to shape how people perceive and interpret contested global events, both domestically and internationally. Strategic humor aims to make one side's narrative of an event more effective while weakening the other side's narrative. It serves the purpose of shaping controversial events in line with the interests of a state, embarrassing or discrediting other governments and media, highlighting discrepancies between the public and private statements of leaders or politicians, and leveraging them to their advantage. On Twitter, especially during the crisis between Russia and Ukraine, memes have played a significant role. The meaning of "meme" is 'an amusing or interesting item (such as a captioned picture or video) or genre of items that is spread widely online, especially through social media. A meme is an internet communication unit that has formal characteristics of an internet meme. These include featuring an existing or borrowed image or a memetic text or idea. The meme then modifies that image, text, or idea in some way by adding a text, an image, or both. The third characteristic is humor, which can vary from elegant to caustic, vulgar, and dark. Memes can be seen as highly refined tools of rhetoric; memes can convey a complex message in a very simple way. To achieve success, memes need to carry significance for a wide range of social media users from diverse backgrounds. For this reason, memes mostly contain popular culture such as movies, TV shows, and books that are known to everyone. In some cases, only popular culture elements belonging to a particular culture are used, while sometimes items known throughout the world are used. From the first weeks of the Russia-Ukraine War, war memes were systematically produced and shared by users on Twitter. These memes discussed war from various angles and offered commentary on war events, diplomatic work, civic experiences, economic effects, and even the memes themselves were sometimes the subject of memes. During the Russia-Ukraine War, the memes shared on Twitter generally serve different purposes for different audiences. There are three major types of audiences: Ukrainians, Westerners, and Russians. For Ukrainians, memes provide a humorous relief from the harsh realities of the war and uplift their spirits. For Western viewers, Ukrainian memes, when appealing to them, help shape public opinion and attempt to gain support from politicians. For Russians, memes are seen as a natural extension of the crisis happening in the real world and are perceived as a weapon on Twitter. The "Ukraine Memes Forces" account stands out when it comes to the strategic use of memes. This account, opened on February 23, 2022, describes itself as the "Source of the best Ukrainian  memes." Since its inception, it has gained over 360,000 followers. Among its followers are academics, journalists from prominent publications, diplomats, advisors to world leaders, and social media managers from multilateral institutions. By sharing memes, the account can reach global elites and serve as an example of how memes can be integrated into strategic communications. The  tweet of Ukraine account dated 7 December 2021 The reason for the effectiveness of memes is that they can be both open-ended and closed-ended, allowing for various interpretations. The inclusion of elements from popular culture also contributes to the spread of memes. However, excessive use of memes, particularly by official Twitter accounts, can have negative consequences. Firstly, there is the issue of portraying war-related memes in a humorous manner to Twitter users. Instead of focusing on the destruction, suffering, death, and pain of war, it may create the impression that Ukraine does not take the seriousness of the war seriously. Memes shared by official state Twitter accounts of Ukraine can contribute to the perception that the war is being treated as insignificant. If the perception that wars are insignificant continues, we may expect new wars and intense conflicts in the near future. Secondly, there is the influence of memes on leaders. Memes about Ukrainian President Zelenskyy portray him as a hero. In tweets comparing Russian and Ukrainian presidents, the Ukrainian president is referred to as a "leader," while the Russian president is labeled a "loser." Memes depict Zelenskyy as a good leader, while memes about Putin consistently create a negative image of him as a leader and an enemy. In particular, the meme shared on February 24, 2022, the first day of the war, featuring Putin and Hitler, with the caption "This is not a 'meme’” but our and your reality right now," can contribute to the perception that Putin is a bad leader. Furthermore, this meme receiving over 1.7 million likes and being retweeted more than 282,000 times can damage Putin's global image. As a result, in order to strengthen his global image, it may lead to further escalation of relations both in Russia and Ukraine. Thirdly, there is the impact of memes on real-life facts. Memes, particularly by simplifying complex processes, reduce the war between Russia and Ukraine to a battle between superheroes and villains. This can lead users to overlook the complexity of real events and propose unrealistic solutions. Figure  1 4. Image Management To persuade foreign populations, it is necessary to create a sympathetic impact on them. In order to generate soft power, states can develop strategies such as image building and branding, undermining the images of others, promoting commonalities and values, and creating empathy through famous personalities. States actively use social media tools to manage their country's international image, eliminate any negative perceptions, explain their political actions, and convey them to the masses in a positive manner. Especially in crisis situations, they resort to different methods to portray their image positively. Traditional mass communication channels also play important roles in this regard. However, this study focuses on diplomacy, image management, and propaganda practices conducted through social media. In this part of the study, discourse analysis technique was employed to analyze the posts shared by the official Twitter accounts of the Embassy of Ukraine in Ankara (@UKRinTR) and the  Embassy of the Russian Federation in Turkey (@RusEmbTurkey). There are multiple reasons for choosing Turkey in this section of the study. Firstly, Turkey has both historical and cultural ties with both Russia and Ukraine. Russia and Ukraine try to create a positive image of Turkey by leveraging these shared cultural and historical bonds. Secondly, Turkey's role as a mediator in the conflict between Russia and Ukraine can make both countries try to attract Turkey based on their own justifications. While Ukraine seeks assistance and support from Turkey against Russia, which has occupied a significant portion of its territory with a large armed force, Russia seeks to legitimize the invasion of Ukraine, which has caused international reactions, and defend itself against severe criticisms within the Turkish public opinion, through the same platform. Turkey's efforts to remain neutral and maintain relations with both Russia and Ukraine make the image-building activities of both countries' governments significant for the Turkish public. The population of the research consists of Twitter posts within a one-year period from February 24, 2022, to February 24, 2023. The sample consists of the top ten Turkish textual content with the most likes. These contents are solely produced by the existing accounts, and retweets of content produced by other accounts are not included in the data. Twitonomy.com website, commonly used for social media analysis, and Twitter's own filtering feature were used for data collection.   Russian Embassy in Ankara Tweets Figure  1 5. During the war, the Ukrainian Embassy and the Russian Embassy, recognizing the critical importance of directing international public support, focused on building good relations with the Turkish people. They produced numerous  contents  to gain public support in line with this objective. In this context, the mentioned account has concentrated on content that focuses on Turkey. Ukrainian Ambassador to Ankara, Vasyl Bodnar, condemned the expulsion in a video message. On May 19, 2022, Ambassador Vasyl Bodnar also celebrated the Turkish people's Youth and Sports Day, which commemorates Atatürk. In a video shared on his Twitter account, Ambassador Bodnar conveyed a message from Ukrainian orphan children in which they celebrated both the Youth and Sports Day and Vyshyvanka Day, a traditional cultural holiday in Ukraine. Similarly, the Russian Embassy accounts also produced content to develop good relations with the Turkish people. For example, the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs celebrated the Turkish people's National Sovereignty and Children's Day on April 23, 2022. When the Twitter accounts of the Turkish embassies of Russia and Ukraine are examined, it can be said that Ukraine is more successful in creating a positive image among the Turkish people compared to Russia. Both in terms of followers and interaction numbers, Ukraine's Twitter account stands out more prominently compared to Russia. Looking at the number of followers, the Ukrainian Embassy account has 119.7 followers, while the Russian Embassy account has 47,501 followers. Furthermore, when we look at the most liked tweet posts, Ukraine's post receives 71.3 thousand likes, while Russia's post receives 1,554 likes. However, it should be noted that the  efforts of social media companies to restrict access to Russia's accounts and the sharing of pro-Russian content, as well as the influence of pro-Ukrainian policies, also play a role in this. Both accounts have made almost all of their posts in the Turkish language. However, they have shared their posts with their followers using different language styles. The Russian Embassy account uses a more formal and serious language in its posts, while the Ukrainian Embassy account shares tweets that are informal and in line with the nature of social media, using a friendly tone to inform its followers. It can be inferred that appealing to Turkish followers' concepts, proverbs, and values has captured the interest of Turkish followers. At this point, referencing the values ​​that the Turkish society possesses has become a facilitating factor in creating a positive image. Findings regarding hashtag usage also reveal that the Ukrainian Embassy Twitter page is significantly ahead of the Russian Embassy page. The Ukrainian Embassy uses relevant and original hashtags in every post, especially actively using the #StandWithUkraine hashtag, while the Russian Embassy rarely resorts to using hashtags, indicating that this feature provided by the Twitter application is not fully utilized. During the war period, the Russian and Ukrainian Embassy accounts created content targeting the Turkish people in order to establish a positive perception. Among these, they celebrated Turkey's important holidays and tried to create a perception of "friendliness" towards the Turkish people with the aid provided after earthquakes. In addition, the Russian Embassy attempts to support its claim that Ukraine is not a "friend" of Turkey by sharing news articles in the media. On the other hand, the Ukrainian Embassy account tries to create a negative image of Russia among the Turkish people by referring to Russia as "enemy," "Russian occupiers," and "orcs" in its posts. The reason these tweets from the Ukrainian Embassy receive so many likes can be attributed to the fact that they share their emotions more perceptibly in their tweets. Another finding from this study is that the Ukrainian Ambassador to Turkey, Vasyl Bodnar, has a personal Twitter account, while the Russian Ambassador to Turkey, Alexey Erkhov, does not have a personal Twitter account. In this sense, the embassy's official social media account differs from the ambassador's personal social media account. The absence of personal Twitter accounts for ambassadors does not create a positive image in terms of digital diplomacy activities. Ambassadors directly communicating with people on their Twitter accounts helps to create an image that is more transparent and democratic in relation to their country. 3.3. Disinformation In international arena, social media is increasingly playing a crucial role in conflicts, crises, and wartime situations. . Especially during the peak of international crises, people tend to share more content related to the issue through their social media accounts.  Conversations on social media often evolve into a duel between citizens of countries in crisis. Due to the wide social participation, disinformation is frequently encountered in such networks.  During this process, especially due to the slow and incomplete flow of accurate information, deceptive messages can easily spread, leading to the use of fake news by conflicting parties in order to mobilize their own public opinion or cause harm to the enemy group.  In the developments regarding the crisis that began between Russia and Ukraine on February 24, 2022, it was observed that the public particularly relied on Twitter to obtain information. However, despite the use of Twitter as an information platform, it is also possible to come across news containing disinformation. Therefore, there are verification platforms established to confirm the authenticity of misinformation and fake news in social media and the media. These platforms, which aim to combat disinformation and misinformation, can be established by individuals, news organizations, civil society organizations, or public broadcasters. The methods employed by verification initiatives are important when investigating suspicious information and determining its accuracy or falsehood. The International Fact-Checking Network (IFCN), an organization with many verification organizations as members and signatories worldwide, has established five fundamental criteria for the credibility of verification initiatives. These criteria include impartiality and fairness, source transparency, financial transparency of sources and organizations, method transparency, and commitment to an open and honest correction policy. With the onset of the war, it was observed that ordinary social media users, in addition to official institutions of Russia and Ukraine, intentionally or unintentionally engaged in disinformation activities beyond propaganda on Twitter. Therefore, in this part of the study, 115 examples of disinformation content tweets, examined by fact-checking websites verified by IFCN such as France24, Teyit, PolitiFact, and StopFake, were collected for the period between February 24, 2022, and February 24, 2023. It has been observed that text-based false news is retweeted less compared to video and photo-based news. The reason for this is that Twitter users are more inclined to believe the authenticity of  visually oriented  content. Accounts disseminating false information generally tend to have higher engagement than accounts sharing accurate information. It is observed that the verification of these news articles by fact-checking sites does not generate as much engagement as the false news itself. Conclusion Information and communications technology   tools have always held a significant place in our lives. While developments during World War II were followed through radio, the Vietnam War was widely observed through television. The emergence of Web 2.0, driven by globalization following the Cold War, along with the subsequent digitalization, facilitated the possibility of interactive communication in the virtual realm, subsequently influencing diplomacy. Traditional diplomacy conducted through conventional mass communication mediums has transformed into a framework encompassing participatory diplomacy, enabled by new communication technologies. This process, referred to as digital diplomacy, encompasses the utilization of various digital tools and social media platforms. In today’s  world,  social media tools are extensively utilized in digital diplomacy, enabling communication not only between states but also among individuals. Digital diplomacy has led to the eradication of temporal and spatial barriers, giving rise to a concept of diplomacy that can be applied "anytime, anywhere, and by anyone." As a result, the public, traditionally the subject of diplomacy, has become an active participant in digital diplomacy, facilitating the ease of information flow. There is no universally accepted definition for digital diplomacy, leading scholars to propose various terms such as "cyber diplomacy," "e-diplomacy," and "networked diplomacy" in an attempt to describe it. Each state implements its own digital diplomacy strategies and establishes dedicated units within their governmental frameworks, demonstrating the absence of constraints in the application of digital diplomacy among nations. There is no consensus on a common definition for digital diplomacy, as well as whether it is a part of public diplomacy.   Those who advocate its inclusion in public diplomacy argue that it should be considered as a part of public diplomacy because the target audience is the same.   They use this similarity as evidence for their claim. On the other hand, those who oppose this view argue that digital diplomacy should not be limited solely to public diplomacy, as it also serves in other areas. They emphasize that it would be incorrect to confine it exclusively to public diplomacy due to its involvement in broader fields. Digital diplomacy, by being integrated into traditional diplomacy, enables the state narratives to spread more easily and quickly, facilitates the establishment of good relations with the society, and allows diplomacy to be conducted in the virtual realm. Compared to diplomacy conducted from embassy buildings, digital diplomacy provides the opportunity to reach much larger audiences with significantly lower costs and efforts. Twitter, the most important tool of digital diplomacy, has become absolutely undeniable today, with its ever-increasing power in all areas of politics. The fact that 98% of UN members have a Twitter account today is an important proof of this. The concept of “Twiplomacy” has emerged as  a result of the active use of Twitter by state leaders and foreign ministries in the execution of foreign policy. The reason why Twitter has become a tool of diplomacy compared to other social media platforms is that it allows to reach large masses thanks to its one-sided following feature. However, with the change of the opening question on Twitter, the news feature has come to the fore. The fact that Twitter is an important tool of digital diplomacy and the "unfollow" and "follow" functions on Twitter cause diplomatic tension between countries and are effective in repairing relations between states and virtual embassies opened on Twitter. While Twitter is actively used in Twiplomacy by state leaders, foreign ministries, and diplomats to fulfill their foreign policies, it is challenging for it to replace traditional diplomacy. The resolution of problems and crises between states cannot be realistically achieved through 140-character messages on Twitter. Twiplomacy serves as a diplomatic tool used to achieve diplomatic objectives, but it is not a comprehensive substitute for traditional diplomacy. Twiplomacy, which has become an important tool in digital diplomacy, offers advantages such as facilitating communication between public officials and different state leaders, serving as a response mechanism during crises, and assisting countries in building their national brand. However, it also presents disadvantages due to the lack of content moderation on Twitter. These disadvantages include an increase in protests, diplomatic crises caused by public officials engaging in behavior they wouldn't exhibit face-to-face, rapid and potentially misinterpreted reactions to events, and issues such as cyberbullying. It is important to note that many of these disadvantages are a result of user behavior rather than inherent flaws in Twitter itself. Strategies can be developed to mitigate these user-induced disadvantages. Some of these strategies include decision-makers seeking the input and approval of relevant advisors before making direct and targeted posts related to foreign policy matters, as well as making it a rule for chief advisers and diplomats to undergo routine social media training. By implementing such measures, it is possible to minimize the negative impacts associated with Twiplomacy and ensure responsible and effective use of Twitter as a diplomatic tool. At the second chapter of the study, the use of Twiplomacy during crises such as the Arab Spring, the Brunson Crisis, and the Russia-Ukraine War was analyzed. During the Arab Spring movement, which started in Tunisia and later spread to other countries in the Middle East and North Africa, Twitter played a significant role alongside Facebook. Twitter acted as an accelerator in mobilizing social groups and served as an information source for protesters. Additionally, social media provided individuals with the ability to globally broadcast protests and events, encouraging political participation. However, while Twitter and Facebook played a prominent role in the initiation and spread of the Arab Spring movement, it is important to note that the underlying causes that triggered the  movement were primarily economic poverty, unemployment, suppression of opposition, and corruption. Social media served as a facilitator for populations already on the verge of explosion. It was an instrumental factor, but not the sole cause of the movement. It is essential to consider the complex dynamics and underlying factors that contribute to such crises, and the role of social media platforms like Twitter and Facebook should be seen as part of a larger context rather than the sole driver of these events. In the Brunson Crisis, a diplomatic crisis that lasted for approximately two years between US and Turkish government officials, it can be said that Twitter was used by both parties to facilitate the flow of information regarding the incident. Furthermore, US officials effectively utilized Twitter to exert pressure on Turkey. When examining the usage of Twitter throughout the period, it can be observed that US officials tweeted after each development to inform the public, while Turkish officials preferred to use Twitter less frequently and relied more on traditional diplomatic tools compared to their US counterparts. Moreover, during the course of the crisis, political actors adopted different languages and styles when utilizing Twitter diplomacy. Two distinct examples representing this are Trump and Erdogan. Trump's language on Twitter is informal, candid, enthusiastic, and has a more personal tone, deviating from formality. It is important to note that the usage patterns and language employed on Twitter by political actors can vary significantly, reflecting their individual communication styles and preferences. During the Russia-Ukraine War, following the outbreak of hostilities, Ukrainian Prime Minister Volodymyr Zelensky and Ukrainian Minister for Digital Transportation Mykhailo Fedorov used their Twitter accounts to call for social media platforms and major companies to withdraw from Russia due to its military operation. This situation also influenced Russia's direct use of Twiplomacy. Although Russia's Twitter usage was limited during this period, both sides utilized Twitter for shaping their state narratives and propagandistic purposes. As a result, it can be said that Twiplomacy is used in crisis situations for different actors and for different purposes. Considering the common aspects, Twiplomacy allows real-time communication in crisis situations. In this way, information flow is provided instantly. At the third part of the study, an attempt was made to illustrate the trends and challenges in international Twiplomacy by using the Ukraine-Russia War as a case study. The emerging results indicate that strategic humor, particularly in the form of memes, played a significant role during the Ukraine-Russia War. The effectiveness of memes can be attributed to the sharing of content that incorporates popular culture, which is both open to interpretation and familiar to everyone, thereby facilitating quicker retweets of the content. However, while the continuous sharing of memes by ordinary users can be seen as a supportive gesture, the constant sharing of memes by  official state accounts may lead to the perception that the government itself does not take the war seriously. Another trend examined in the study is the image management of states. During international crises, states employ various strategies to legitimize their actions and create a positive perception. The Russian and Ukrainian embassies in Turkey have implemented various strategies to establish a positive image on the neutral grounds of Turkey. When analyzing the social media posts of the Russian and Ukrainian embassies in Ankara, it can be said that Ukraine has been more successful in creating a positive image among the Turkish people compared to Russia. One reason for the Ukrainian Embassy's relatively higher success is that they share real-time developments from the frontlines with visual content, whereas the Russian side predominantly relies on statements from government officials. Additionally, while the Ukrainian Embassy's posts include everyday topics and elements of Turkish culture, the Russian Embassy's use of an official language led to a lack of sufficient interest from the Turkish public. The absence of a Twitter account for the Russian Embassy, while the Ukrainian Ambassador has one, also created difficulties in building a positive image for the Russian Embassy. The reason is that they have observed a preference for engaging in dialogue with a human rather than a machine on Twitter, and therefore, the presence of Ambassadors' Twitter accounts plays a role in fulfilling their digital diplomacy strategies. In Twiplomacy, the greatest challenge encountered during crisis processes is disinformation. While traditional media used to be the realm of media professionals who produced and disseminated content, nowadays, every internet and social media user has the ability to create and spread content in the new media, which has led to such a challenge. During times of crisis and war, disinformation is often used not only to undermine each other but also to capture the attention of the masses. It has been observed that disinformation-based news spread through Twitter plays a role in the dissemination of such news by social media users, as well as by official Twitter accounts of government leaders and politicians. The primary reasons for this spread can be attributed to the desire for quick access to and dissemination of information, reluctance to engage in research, lack of developed skepticism reflexes, and attachment to one's own beliefs or biases. Particularly, content that reflects one's own thoughts and biases facilitates the spread of disinformation as it is perceived to be true by those who share similar disinformation. On the other hand, due to the real-time nature embraced in digital diplomacy and Twiplomacy, the dissemination of verified/falsified factual information remains lower compared to the audiences reached by once-shared information. To be resilient against disinformation during crisis processes, it is necessary to have a skeptical and critical mindset, question the credibility of sources and content when encountering suspicious  information, and use Twitter slowly, waiting for new developments and information to emerge rather than amplifying sensational news interactions. Based on the findings obtained in the study, Twiplomacy, as a subcategory of digital diplomacy, assists during crises by facilitating instant information sharing by state leaders, ministers, and the masses, global dissemination of the crisis, reaching wide audiences, and enabling direct communication. When examining the strategies implemented by states during crisis processes and the challenges they face, it can be generally stated that visual elements play a significant role. This is because users perceive visual elements more quickly than written texts, and users tend to believe tweets they watch and listen to as videos more than those they read.  Thus, the importance of visual elements becomes evident during crisis processes   Particularly, when such tweets are posted by official government authorities, users are more inclined to believe them.
